data.title: Aurora Innovation: Autonomous Trucking Nears Commercial Inflection
data.description:
Aurora Innovation's (AUR) stock appears to have found a floor over the past 6 months as momentum in the underlying business begins to build. I previously suggested that Aurora was making material progress in its attempt to scale driverless freight operations. This has continued in recent months, with driverless miles and deployed capabilities both increasing. While the early signs are positive, it will be late 2026 at the earliest before Aurora really demonstrates its ability to scale driverless operations though.

Aurora's stock has performed poorly since listing, but the next 12 months could be more constructive due to the accelerated deployment of driverless trucks. It will likely will be 2028 before Aurora's revenue multiple begins to look more reasonable, although this matters little, as the company has a long growth runway and just needs to demonstrate its ability to scale.

Market Conditions

While macro uncertainty is elevated at the moment, and diesel prices could cause disruptions to the freight industry, Aurora's macro exposure is limited at the moment due to a lack of scale. A severe downturn could create caution amongst potential customers, but Aurora's growth is likely to remain dictated by supply rather than demand.

On the supply side, Aurora is making progress with its manufacturing partnerships and continues to expand its operating envelope. The company has suggested that its addressable market is now over 3.6 billion vehicle miles.

Aurora Innovation

Aurora surpassed 250,000 driverless miles in January, suggesting that the company's driverless operations have continued to ramp in recent months. It is unclear how many driverless vehicles the company now has on the road, but Aurora was previously targeting 10 driverless vehicles by the end of 2025. It is also worth noting that Aurora still has an observer in its vehicles due to partner requirements. Aurora is now targeting 200 driverless trucks in operation by the end of 2026 with deployment weighted to the back half. While this would already represent a step change in scale, industrialized scaling isn't expected until 2027.

Growth is supported by the fact that the Aurora Driver is now capable of driverless operations in a range of inclement weather (rain, fog and heavy wind). While this may seem like a fairly minor update, Aurora estimates that it was unable to operate around 40% of the time in 2025 due to weather conditions. The company continues to invest in inclement weather capabilities, and is collecting data in the Rockies so that it can eventually deploy its trucks in snowy conditions.

Aurora's latest software release also expands driverless operations to multiple new lanes, including El Paso to Fort Worth. In addition, Aurora is now conducting supervised autonomous operations between Dallas and Laredo, which is an important freight route for trade between the US and Mexico. Aurora also believes that its system is now sufficiently generalized to enable expansion across the Sunbelt.

Aurora is also making meaningful progress automating the creation of new content for the Aurora Atlas. Aurora's Verifiable AI system is used to automatically generate semantic components of the Aurora Atlas, significantly reducing human labor requirements. As a result, the expansion of the Aurora Atlas is expected to accelerate going forward.

Aurora is hauling sand 20 hours a day for Detmar. Most of the 60-mile route is on the highway though, making this a significantly different operation to Kodiak's frac sand hauling. Detmar is expected to transition to driverless vehicles in Q2 when Aurora deploys its second fleet of driverless trucks. The company also has plans to leverage its technology in areas like agriculture and mining, drones.

On the manufacturing front, the first Volvo autonomous trucks recently rolled off the pilot line, supporting deployment at scale. Aurora's second-generation hardware kit is also expected in the next few months. The cost of the components in this kit will be roughly halved, while durability is expected to triple. This hardware will also enable driverless operations without an observer.

Financial Analysis

Aurora generated roughly 3 million USD revenue in 2025 which was weighted to the back half of the year, with fourth quarter revenue increasing 25% sequentially to 1 million USD.

Aurora expects 14-16 million USD revenue in 2026, which will be heavily back-loaded. The company is also targeting an 80 million USD revenue rate exiting 2026 from a fleet of more than 200 driverless vehicles (no observer).

Initial revenue comes from a Transportation-as-a-Service model, with Aurora transitioning to a Driver-as a-Service model in 2027. Under TaaS, Aurora owns the fleet and provides the freight service. In comparison, customers own the fleet in the DaaS model with Aurora providing the self-driving technology. Aurora expects its TaaS fleet to remain below 500 trucks.

Aurora's operating loss totaled 238 million USD in Q4 while operating cash burn was 146 million USD and CapEx was 8 million USD. Despite this, Aurora's liquidity is still almost 1.5 billion USD which the company believes will be sufficient for it to reach breakeven in 2028.

Quarterly cash burn is expected to be in the 190-220 million USD range on average in 2026 and Aurora is targeting breakeven gross margins on a run rate basis exiting the year. While reduced hardware costs will help with this, Aurora also needs to reduce the burn of remote assistance and on-site support. It is unclear where the company's current performance sits, but it is likely in the high cost part of the curves below. For comparison, Pony has suggested that it is on target to achieve 1 remote assistant for every 30 vehicles, although the time frame for this isn't clear.

Conclusion

Pressure on SaaS stocks in 2026 demonstrate that the market is starting to more broadly price in the impact of AI. Self-driving vehicle companies are yet to really benefit from this though, which is somewhat surprising given that operations are scaling.

There are limited options to get clean and direct exposure to self-driving technology at the moment. Stocks like Alphabet (GOOG), Tesla (TSLA) and Mobileye (MBLY) are driven in large part by their non-self-driving businesses, and Chinese companies like Pony and WeRide expose investors to significant risk, independent of business fundamentals.

As a result, Aurora is perhaps one of the best ways to gain exposure to the proliferation of self-driving vehicles that is now happening. 2026 will be make or break in terms of the company's ability to scale driverless operations, and success would likely see the stock move significantly higher.

While Aurora is becoming an interesting opportunity, uncertainty regarding how the autonomous trucking will evolve remains high. There are a number of reasonably well resourced competitors that are all at similar stages of maturity. In addition, Waymo and Tesla are likely to target the freight market in time. This could be problematic as competition will largely be based on price and I don't expect there to be a meaningful first mover advantage.

data.title: Adobe: Cheap For All The Wrong Reasons
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Cavan Tang as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Rating: Buy

Price target: $422.32

Browse through social media long enough and you'll start to understand why Adobe (ADBE) trades at where it does. The output quality of generative AI has improved at a pace that's hard to overstate, to the point where it's getting difficult to distinguish what is real from what is synthetic.

A long-only equity investor looking at this may wonder, why pay a premium for a suite of tools that can seemingly be replicated for free? That question is what has driven Adobe's forward P/E multiple from the mid-40s into just slightly above 10. It is also, I'd argue, the wrong question entirely.

The market is asking whether Photoshop, Premiere, and the like can compete with generative AI, or will it be replaced entirely when the more relevant question is whether anything can displace the integrated system Adobe has built: the integrated workflows, format standards, and institutional standardization that make switching costs not only high but operationally disruptive, and nothing has emerged to change that. For that reason, I rate Adobe a buy.

Content supply chain: an invisible infrastructure

The majority of discussions about Adobe's business revolve around their industry leading software for digital media, less so about their file formats, and almost never about the invisible infrastructure that binds the entire digital content supply chain together.

The content supply chain begins with ideation and concept development and ends with analytics and the feedback loop. While the diagram above shows a linear process, in practice it's a continuous iterative process where the feedback informs revisions upstream for better marketing outcomes.

When something is created in Adobe, every decision or edit about that file travels through the entire workflow with nothing lost, ensuring information continuity and consistency from the draft to the final version.

Operational drivers: Record new digital media ARR and rapid AI monetization progress

The market focuses on 2 primary drivers: net new digital media ARR and generative credit revenue. These are well covered, so I will go into them briefly before expanding on the lesser discussed question of the existing business' durability and whether disruption fears are applied with the correct lens.

Net new digital media ARR

Digital Media ARR exits the year at $19.2B, representing a year-on-year growth of 11.5%, alongside full-year revenue of $17.65B at a growth of 11% year-on-year. Full year net new ARR added was an approximate total of $1.98B, with Q4 delivering the largest single quarter of net new ARR in Adobe history at $580M.

ARR is growing faster than recognized revenue suggests upselling to higher-tiered plans. Management confirms that over 75% of net new ARR was due to cross-selling and up-selling and subscription growth.

Growth of net new ARR to a historic record continues to contradict AI disruption fears that began with ChatGPT's debut back in 2022. If product-level disruption fears were manifesting into actual customer decisions, there should be ARR deterioration. Net new ARR is refusing to confirm the bear thesis, for now.

Generative credit: the next billion-dollar business

Generative credits were introduced in 2023 as a mechanism to measure engagement with Adobe's AI functions but lacked meaningful monetisation enforcement until June 2025. That enforcement date is where we should focus for tracking AI revenue contribution.

Progress since then has been rapid. By Q3 FY2025, AI-first ARR had already exceeded management's end-of-year target of $250 million within the first full period of enforcement, a notable achievement given the timeline.

By Q4 FY2025, generative credits appeared for the first time in management's prepared remarks as an explicit ARR driver alongside Creative Cloud strength, signalling a structural shift in how management communicates monetisation progress. The 3x quarter over quarter jump in credit consumption coinciding with record net new ARR in that quarter is worth noting.

Q1 FY2026 marked an inflection. Generative credit consumption became one of three headline KPIs used by management to demonstrate strategic execution. For the first time, Firefly ending ARR was stated explicitly at $250 million, directly linking credit monetisation to a dollar figure. The absolute size remains modest relative to total Digital Media ARR of $26 billion, but the trajectory is clear.

Product level vs. System level

From the previous section, we can surmise that generative credit is still in the early stages of monetization, built on top of a subscription base that has shown no signs of structural deterioration. But this raises the question the market has been grappling with since 2022, what makes that foundational layer so durable?

The disruption narrative has an answer: it's not durable, and deterioration is coming. Canva's user-friendly interfaces are eroding market share at the lower end. Generative AI can replicate certain creative outputs; no need for Adobe's tools. AI productivity gains mean fewer seats are needed to produce the same volume of work.

These concerns are valid; the mistake here is not the identification of the problems, but that it implies that Adobe is a collection of software products competing on features. Adobe is more precisely understood as the operating system that transforms creative output into marketing and business outcomes. Uninstalling the default browser and installing Chrome is fundamentally different from changing your laptop's operating system.

Operating system, operating…

Consider Qualcomm's decision in Sept 2025 to use Adobe to accelerate parts of their content supply chain. A campaign brief enters their system through Adobe Workfront, which coordinates work across marketing and creative teams.

Creative assets are generated through Adobe Firefly, trained on Qualcomm's own intellectual property, ensuring consistent brand identity and differentiation from competitors.

Those assets flow into Adobe Experience Manager, a living library of branded assets each carrying embedded metadata, usage rights, and campaign history that cannot cleanly migrate, where variations of the content can be generated and tested against different target personas.

Regional teams adapt them into a local context through Adobe Express using pre-approved templates from Adobe GenStudio.

Insights from previous campaigns feed back through Marketo Engage into the next brief for better targeting and customer experience.

Content flows naturally downstream because every stage operates within the Adobe ecosystem. Replacing any single node introduces friction. Replacing everything means rebuilding your entire marketing infrastructure from scratch. What gets left behind is a bespoke AI model trained on years of proprietary data alongside a vast collection of assets that cannot be migrated without meaningful risk of incompatibility.

Qualcomm is not the only enterprise deepening its Adobe investments. Over 40% of Adobe's top 50 enterprise accounts doubled their ARR spend since FY23. Customers with ARR over $10M grew 20% year over year in Q1 FY26. Numbers like these are not consistent with disruption at the margins. They look more like an operating system becoming more entrenched, one that even its most credible challenger ultimately chose to join rather than displace.

Figma acquisition

In 2023, Adobe's $20B acquisition for Figma imploded under regulatory pressure, but not before Figma accepted. Paradoxically, the strongest evidence for the bear case's emerging threat argument also confirms its most revealing weakness. A company that genuinely believed it could displace Adobe would not sell under any circumstance. Acceptance meant Figma's own leadership understood their ceiling. They were building a better collaborative design tool, not a replacement content supply chain.

Regulators blocked the deal specifically because they identified Figma as the most significant emerging competitive threat to Adobe's design tools. This inadvertently confirmed the same thing: the strongest challenger self-assessed as insufficient to win independently and was competing on a single node rather than the system. No competing platform thus far spans both creative and marketing sides of the equation simultaneously.

Financial Analysis

The following analysis showcases 3 scenarios for Adobe's financial trajectory through 2030. All 3 share identical 2026E, reflecting tight near-term visibility. Divergence begins in 2027E, where assumptions around AI monetisation, competitive pressures, and margin profiles differ materially.

A note on FCF methodology: the model uses a simplified cash flow build that excludes deferred revenue movements and SBC from operating cash flows, which are material to Adobe as a subscription business. The result is a systematic understatement of FCF across all historical and forecast periods. As a reference point, Adobe's reported FY2025 FCF of $9.85B exceeded the model's estimate of $6.7B by approximately 47%. FCF figures and DCF-derived price targets throughout this analysis should therefore be interpreted as conservative estimates. The true intrinsic value implied by Adobe's actual cash generation is likely higher across all scenarios.

This analysis uses three methods to triangulate the intrinsic value of Adobe: P/E, P/R, and DCF. The P/E method uses a PEG-based approach, which adjusts for growth rates that P/E ignores, applying a 1.5x PEG ratio to FY2030E EPS of $36.61 at a growth rate of 14.01%, implying a 21x P/E and a 2030 price target of $769.38 discounted back to $515.29 today. The P/R method applies 7.5x FY2030E revenue of $39.23 billion, discounted back to $542.38 today, providing a cross-check on the P/E result that is independent of margin assumptions. The DCF applies a WACC of 10.5% and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, yielding $269.30. The average of the three methodologies produces a blended price target of $442.32

Despite bear case assumptions of slowing revenue growth, margin compressions from elevated AI investment, and disappointing generative credit monetization, Adobe continues to grow non-GAAP EPS to $31.79 and FCF to $8.78B by 2030.

On the surface, the growth rate deceleration from 23% during pandemic peaks down to 10-11% in recent years seemingly confirms the bear narratives. For investors focused on headline growth rates, there is more than meets the eye. Segment mix and contracted backlog tell a story of deepening entrenchment, not competitive retreat.

The deceleration in growth rates can be attributed to a few compounding factors. Firstly, the pandemic created demand that didn't exist otherwise, pulling it from future years and leaving a vacuum.

Simultaneously, enterprise CFOs tightened their software budgets as they digested COVID-era technology commitments.

But there is also a third reason worth acknowledging honestly, and it's that Adobe is simply a larger business today.

Sustaining a 20%+ growth on a $23.8B will require an additional $5B in new business each year. A more manageable feat when Adobe was half its size 5 years ago. Despite these challenges, Adobe continued to compound $12.9B to $23.8B over that period.

The headline growth, however, tells only half a story. Beneath it, the composition of that revenue is quietly shifting.

Looking at the individual segments, Digital Media represents the largest share of total revenue.

In 2025, Digital Media generated $17.6B against Digital Experience at $5.9B. Together, they've compounded at 13% CAGR from 2021 to 2025

The more interesting dynamic is not in the absolute sizes of each segment but how their relative contributions have been shifting, with Digital Experience nipping at the heels.

At its peak, the Digital Media segment was 3.3 times larger than the Digital Experience segment. A clear downtrend emerges, reaching a trough of 2.96 before settling at 3.01 in 2025.

The implication here is that the segment traditionally dominated by enterprise demand has been growing faster over this period. Enterprises aren't simply using Adobe's design tools but also expanding into the marketing, activation, and analytics layer.

Unlike individual creative subscriptions, Digital Experience contracts are typically multi-year agreements with significantly larger contract values and greater switching costs. This segment's growing contribution represents a structural strengthening of Adobe's revenue base, not just a mix shift.

The 2024 trough is noteworthy here. Right when Adobe shares are down 25% on AI disruption fears, Digital Experience was recording its highest ever contribution to revenue. The market is pricing in deterioration at the exact moment the data showed deepening entrenchment.

Looking at this from a different angle, the contracted backlog confirms the same, and its changing composition adds further weight to the argument for entrenchment over disruption.

Remaining performance obligations (RPO) represent contracted but unrecognized revenue, with current remaining performance obligations being what converts to revenue within the next 12 months and the cRPO rate representing that proportion.

Total RPO grew from $11.34B in 2020 to $22.52B in 2025 alongside a cRPO rate that has fallen from 73% to 65% over that same period.

Taken together, Adobe's backlog is expanding while enterprises commit further into the future. A combination that is hard to reconcile with a disruption narrative.

The revenue picture tells a story of deepening entrenchment. The margin profile tells two stories. Gross margins confirm that the core economics of the business are improving, while operating margin compression and subsequent recovery suggest the AI investment cycle has already been fully digested.

The bear case argues that AI competition will either force Adobe to lower prices or increase costs to defend its position. This is true, but only for the latter and only modestly.

Gross margins have actually improved from the lows of 87% in 2020 to their peak of 89% in 2025. Driven by cross-sells and upsells as confirmed by management previously.

Improving margins driven by tier upgrades or product add-ons are indicators of customers deepening their commitment rather than reducing it. A customer base under active disruption would be experiencing higher churning and downgrades, which would have the opposite effect.

Peaking at 37% in 2021, operating margins compressed to 31% by 2024, driven by increased R&D investment from 16% to 18% and modest G&A expansion, before recovering fully to 37% in 2025 as spending normalised and gross margins expanded.

The picture provided by the data tells us that the initial spending on AI investments did have an impact on operating margins but has largely stabilised since 2024 with gross margins expanded, G&A and S&M reduced, leaving only ongoing R&D maintenance spending, which led to the operating margins recovery. Adobe did in fact increase spending to defend its competitive position but has managed this well as evidenced by the recovery in operating margins.

The operational picture establishes a few things clearly: Adobe's revenue base is increasingly durable, its cost structure has absorbed an investment cycle and emerged intact, and three years of disruption narrative have failed to show up in the fundamentals. The question that remains is what that business is worth and whether the current market price reflects economic reality.

Before the disruption narrative took hold, the markets assigned Adobe an average forward P/E multiple of 35x. A premium assigned to a business with recurring revenues, high switching costs, and multi-year compounding consistency

At approximately 10x as of today, the current multiple does not reflect a growth scare but a terminal prognosis. It's not pricing in a difficult recovery; it's pricing in none whatsoever.

The natural question to ask is what multiple was used in this analysis to arrive at its price targets. The P/E valuation applies a constant 1.5x PEG ratio across all three scenarios, producing an implied exit multiple of 21x. This represents a 40% discount to the 35x historical average shown above. Divergences between bear, base, and bull price targets of $408, $515, and $565 reflect different operational outcomes, not different multiple assumptions.

To a reader for whom these targets appear aggressive, consider what they actually assume. An implied exit multiple of 21x never recovers the historical average of 35x. It does not assume a re-rate as disruption narratives fade. It assumes a permanent structurally lower multiple. Even under that assumption, the bear case still implies a 70% upside from current levels. From this perspective, the price targets aren't aggressive. The current price is.

Multiples-based approaches carry an inherent limitation; earnings can be embellished by accounting choices, and they're also sensitive to growth assumptions.

Free cash flow, however, is harder to flatter and reaches the same conclusion as the previous methods. Adobe is undervalued.

The chart on the left uses model FCF, which understates the actuals, the trend remains clear. Yields jumped 189% year over year during a period where free cash flow grew 28%.

The striking difference between the yield and free cash flow implies that yield expansion was primarily driven by the stock de-rating rather than the growth of free cash flow.

Comparing Adobe's FCF yield to its own historical average and the broader market, the current yield of 6.54% is 2.35x above its five-year average and 1.74x above the S&P 500 average over the same period.

These figures are derived from model FCF, which systematically understates actuals. Adobe's reported FY2025 FCF of $9.85B implies an actual trailing yield closer to 9%. A growing business generating bond-like cash returns is either a generational buying opportunity or a business in terminal decline. The evidence thus far suggests only one of these is true.

As noted earlier, DCF inputs use the modelled FCF that understate the actual through the exclusion of stock-based compensation and deferred revenue. Therefore, the outputs below are conservative estimates of intrinsic value.

The DCF applies a WACC of 10.5%, derived from a risk-free rate of 4.4% reflecting the prevailing 10-year treasury yields, a beta of 1.30, a broad market return of 9.5%, and a capital structure of 94% equity weighted against an after-tax cost of debt of 3.0% sourced directly from Adobe's bond schedule.

The resulting discount rate implies a meaningful risk premium for a company with stable recurring revenue and positive cash generation. A deliberate conservative assumption that builds on top of conservative FCF inputs already noted.

Based on the sensitivity analysis, a current market price of $240 is most consistent with a WACC of 10.5% and a terminal growth of 2.0%, which implies Adobe generates no real growth in perpetuity. An unlikely scenario given our prior analyses.

At $240, the market is assuming either a terminal growth rate approaching zero real growth, a discount rate above 10.5%, or some combination of both. Regardless of the implied assumptions, the conclusion is clear: the market is pricing Adobe as a business with dim prospects.

The bear case FCF of $8.78B by 2030 is itself derived from understated model inputs and already exceeds what the current price appears to credit Adobe for generating and is pricing outcomes worse than what this analysis can reasonably construct despite being deliberately conservative.

Valuation Summary

A forward P/E of approximately 10x, FCF yields of roughly 9%, and an implied real growth rate approaching zero for a company that had just delivered its largest ever net new Digital Media ARR in Q4 FY25, whose generative credit monetisation is still in its earliest stages of scaling, and whose enterprise customers are not abandoning it but rather deepening their commitment to it, do not make sense. It would only make sense if the market viewed Adobe as a collection of individual products that compete on features. Then you could make a strong argument that generative AI and new entrants pose an existential threat to Adobe. The operational and strategic picture that's needed for this to be true simply does not exist. It's not about individual product features compared to its competitors but how they work together to allow marketing itself to function in today's digital age. Viewed through this lens, there are no contenders. None have come close.

Risks

While I maintain a buy rating on Adobe, an analysis is incomplete without considering the risks that could impair the thesis, particularly those that threaten the system level moat underpinning this valuation.

Rival ecosystem/interoperability

By now it is clear that Adobe's actual moat is the creative ecosystem it has built. A comparable competing ecosystem hasn't existed so far, but if this were to change, it could seriously jeopardize Adobe's competitive position.

The greatest threat to Adobe isn't a better AI model. Better individual tools at any node are irrelevant in isolation, just as a better camera app doesn't threaten Apple's ecosystem.

Apple owns the full stack, hardware, OS, App Store, and services in the same way Adobe owns ideation through to analytics. Android did not emerge from building a better phone, but from Google building the platform layer that allowed a new ecosystem to form around it. The threat to Apple was never about a competitor's better features but an alternative ecosystem with its own network effects.

Analogous to Adobe, the threat isn't Midjourney having better image generation; it's whether something like Android emerges. This could happen through vertical integration, where an incumbent moves upstream towards ideation and content creation, or a coalition of specialists stitching together an ecosystem through partnerships.

Currently, there are already a handful of players that operate in multiple nodes of the content supply chain; they represent the greatest potential for vertical integration, the most direct and likely path towards a competing ecosystem. However, they all share the same problem: coverage without depth.

Microsoft is the furthest along this path, but Designer and Clipchamp are not professional-grade creative tools. Canva is broad but thin. HubSpot is strong at distribution but nascent on creative production. None have reached the same level of depth as Adobe. To do so requires large capital outlays, time, and overcoming Adobe's deeply embedded switching costs.

However, if specialists in each node came together to form a coalition, this largely removes many of the problems a vertical integrator faces but creates a new set of problems centred on interoperability.

On the technical front, how do we ensure that outputs at one node flow cleanly to the next without data loss or corruption risk? To do so, the partners must share proprietary data and architecture to create a seamless bridge between them.

This naturally requires a level of coordination that is operationally difficult. Each company has different engineering priorities, release cycles, and product roadmaps. The stability of a stitched ecosystem is only as strong as its weakest partnership. Building enterprise trust on this foundation is itself another problem.

Neither path has cleared its respective hurdle today. Vertical integrators lack the depth and face the cost and time required to displace Adobe's switching costs. Coalition builders face coordination and interoperability barriers that make enterprise-grade stability difficult to guarantee. Until one of these paths produces a credible end-to-end alternative, the emergence of a competing ecosystem remains a longer-dated structural risk rather than a near-term threat to Adobe's position. For these reasons, I maintain a buy rating.

AI monetisation growth

The primary concern for markets is AI monetisation's ability to scale fast enough to contribute to net new ARR and offset the softening growth of total revenues. This matters because it affects the growth trajectory. If it were slower than anticipated, then reacceleration for all forecasts is pushed outwards, compressing consensus views on price targets.

Q1 FY2026 reports $400 million in Digital Media ARR, which misses consensus estimates of $450-460 million. This is misleading on its own without context because the natural conclusion is that AI monetisation is moving too slow. However, when we consider the surrounding circumstances, the $50-60 million miss seems a lot less alarming.

First, both management and analysts did not anticipate the speed of Adobe's stock image business erosion due to people's increasing preference to use generative AI for ideation and content creation. The decline of this business contributed approximately $78 million to the ARR shortfall. If management hadn't anticipated the speed of this erosion, how could the analysts? And if they had known, would they still have set the same $450-460 million estimate?

Aside from the cannibalization, Adobe's management is focused on growing their freemium MAU for a larger monetizable base in favour of contributions to ARR right now. This naturally dampens near-term ARR since those users have not yet hit paywalls and converted into paid tiers. Notably, AI-first ARR more than tripled year-over-year, signalling that monetisation is working, not stalling.

The real risk is whether the conversion of freemium users accelerates fast enough through the back half of FY2026 to offset Stock business erosion and deliver the ARR reacceleration embedded in our FY2027 assumptions. Q2 FY2026 will be the next meaningful data point to assess whether that trajectory is on track.

Design schools and creative programmes

Adobe owes its dominance partly to the design schools and creative programs that teach using their tools. Students carry this knowledge into the workplace, which reinforces adoption from the bottom up. If curricula begin to shift toward Canva or Figma, then switching costs work in reverse, carrying a preference for those tools into the workplace and potentially influencing procurement decisions at senior levels later on.

This change will likely impact the low- to middle-end of the market first. Canva and Figma have already made inroads at the individual creator and SMB level, where customers have greater flexibility in choice and do not require professional-grade design tools.

The trend is slow-moving by nature. It takes time for curricula to change, then years before that shift shows up in enterprise procurement decisions. With enterprises still largely requiring Adobe's professional-grade tooling and the pipeline from education to enterprise spanning years, this remains a long-cycle watch item rather than a near-term threat to our Buy rating.

Conclusion

In the rush to form a view, the market has used the wrong lens to evaluate Adobe. The results of the market's de-rating are irreconcilable with the operational and strategic realities of the business. Margins are expanding, cash flows are at record levels, and AI monetization signals are accelerating beneath the surface.

The velocity of the de-rating suggests narrative risk was repriced alongside fundamental risk. Narratives taken to an extreme create mispricing opportunities, and the current price reflects exactly that.

At approximately $240, a 66% peak-to-trough decline does not make sense for a business with Adobe's fundamental profile. An investor who buys at current prices is essentially paying a fair price for the bear case and gets both the base and bull cases for free. For these reasons, I maintain a Buy rating with a price target of $422.32.

data.title: German Inflation Increases Further In April
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

It could have been worse. German inflation increased in April, but slightly less than feared, as the inflationary shock currently remains an isolated energy price shock. While the national inflation measure came in at 2.9% year-on-year, from 2.7% YoY in March, the European measure also stood at 2.9% YoY. Core inflation dropped to 2.3% YoY from 2.5% YoY in March.

First inflation wave in full swing and more yet to come

Today’s inflation data shows that the first inflation wave is in full swing. The April increase was almost exclusively driven by higher energy prices. However, knock-on effects on transportation costs, food prices and other industrial products will follow over the coming months. Needless to say, the longer the war in the Middle East and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz last, the higher the likelihood that the initial energy price shock will not only have knock-on effects but could also be accompanied by additional supply chain frictions and, in turn, a self-enhancing inflationary spiral.

In this regard, a lot is currently being said about comparing the current oil price shock with that of 2022. In fact, the surge in inflation in 2022 started already in 2021, when German inflation jumped from 2% YoY to 6% YoY within five months – which illustrates how fast a severe exogenous shock can actually translate into soaring inflation.

However, we still don’t think that we will see a repetition of the 2022 double-digit inflation rates. The current price shock is hitting the German economy in a weaker state than in 2022, and the ability (and willingness) of consumers to actually pay higher prices is clearly limited. Instead, it will be companies in the middle of the supply chain that might be squeezed the most; they'll be unable to fend off higher wholesale prices but also unable to pass these higher costs onto customers.

As a result, German inflation looks set to increase further to some 4% by summer, but any rise beyond that would require even higher oil prices and substantial fiscal stimulus to offset weaker demand.

German inflation data adds to stagflationary evidence ahead of tomorrow's ECB meeting

Turning to the ECB, German inflation data adds to the evidence of increasing stagflationary pressures ahead of tomorrow’s policy meeting. As much as the rise in actual inflation and inflation expectations will fuel the rate hike debate, growing signs of adverse growth effects will make aggressive rate hikes less straightforward.

In this regard, the fact that German core inflation actually dropped should provide the ECB with some comfort, at least in the near term. Looking further ahead, calls for ECB rate hikes will get louder. However, even though the ECB’s primary policy goal is price stability, it’s hard to see that it would really want to fight an exogenous supply shock at the cost of worsening an economic downturn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: AMC Robotics: Related Party Revenue Seems To Be Financing The Business
data.description:
AMC Robotics Corporation (AMCI) is a recently listed SPAC, so there's right away a bit of dilution to consider from options and warrants, though apparently not so much that it seriously caps upside. The bigger issue is the lack of a forward picture since the AI and robotics angle is something that they aren't commercial with yet and are just developing now, so it's very hard to say what cash burn will be in the future, or if there will be cash burn in the future as some of their developing products are supposed to be launched in Q2.

Another thing is that for the existing operations, which are focused on selling security cameras through e-commerce platforms like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), the CEO of AMCI also sits on the other sides of the company's major commercial relationships — that is to say that there are a lot of related party considerations both in revenue and in terms of supply of products for distribution. This makes it even harder to rest assured of the evolution of the financials, since there's no assurance that the CEO will favor AMCI over other companies that AMCI does business with that he also owns. It also seems that its existing security products business is basically being transitioned out, and they will no longer be operating as a distributor of these products quite soon.

So new products will need to support the business, but we aren't sure of the incoming economics of these products. Although, it also seems that a revenue sharing agreement has been put in place to be able to pay AMCI from related party Kami with a revenue share of 30% for as long as those converted subscribers from camera product sales hold. If they stop buying inventory to distribute and then pick up revenue share, cash flows could improve a lot.

Also, they do have $7 million in cash, so they should be able to last another year assuming forward the latest annual cash burn, and perhaps that cash burn is about to shrink to the point where it won't be too much of an issue, though we expect instead mounting R&D, which has been strangely falling, not rising, despite products in the pipeline, and also a new manufacturing hub in Vietnam is likely going to draw on cash too, announced in January. As said, it's unclear how the number will turn in the near future and what the capital needs of the business will be. It is safer to assume that more financing will be needed, and that it's uncertain whether any of the developments will be commercially successful.

Discussion of Current Results

There is a fair bit to discuss. The first is the pretty dramatic change in the sales picture.

Product revenues are down deliberately. They are trying to turnover and distribute products, specifically security cameras, in a higher margin way. Moreover, these sales are supposed to convert customers onto a related party product, a software of a company called Kami also controlled by AMCI's CEO, and share in the revenues from those software sales. There are terms to these sharing arrangements that have improved of late:

Note that the terms have become more generous. That is the CEO deciding to sacrifice flows at Kami and flood them into AMCI. This is good for AMCI shareholders but it also reflects how dependent shareholders are on how these related party moves are decided. In this case, Kami is giving up economics in favor of AMCI.

The associated improvement of economics can be seen above in the significant gross margin lift from greater share of revenue sharing revenues from Kami, the related party.

Forward Considerations

Note that the company is working on Kyro, which is a quadruped robot that is supposed to supplement security cameras by being dispatched to the anomalous location and providing real time surveillance. Then there's the NovaArm, the company's logistics robot. The latter is supposed to become commercial in Q2, the former has a manufacturing base now in Vietnam established after the FY close of books to our understanding, and they did a partnership for GPU compute to enhance their robotics with AI, and this is ultimately where interest in the stock would come from the physicalization of AI for specific purposes. However, despite the developments, R&D expenses are falling. It's the opposite of what you would expect.

While they are gearing up for at least the NovaArm's hopefully profitable release, it seems that selling the security cameras is being sunsetted, or that the economic terms of distributing the cameras might change (as per the 10-K):

entities have authorized the Company to utilize their e-commerce platform accounts free of charge until October 20, 2026. The Authorization Agreements with Xiaoyun and Yishijue will continue until the existing inventory of the Company’s products has been sold, at which time the agreements will be terminated.

At the point of termination, we aren't sure if the former selling business will continue or whether the economic terms for using these ecommerce seller accounts will simply change. On the latter, we'd be unsure by how much.

Other than Kami, which is sharing revenue with AMCI, there is another related party which is Senslab, in which the CEO of AMCI has a 38% stake. This is where they get their security cameras from that they are currently selling:

During the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, 100% of inventories, were purchased from related parties, Senslab HK Limited and its subsidiary Senslab Technology Co., Ltd (collectively referred to as “Senslab”). Refer to Note 13 “Concentration Risk” for purchases from each supplier under Supplier Concentration. AMC Robotics Corporation has secured favorable payment terms with Senslab, extending the payment period from 60 days to 180 days

While it's nice that they have better terms, this is their major cash outflow and again its determined through related parties, and not arm's length. It's hard to know what to make of this, but perhaps it's not a long-term consideration if the security camera distribution activity is going to be phased out.

But if that's the case then where will the cash inflows be coming from? Supposedly, from the new Kami revenue sharing at 30% in perpetuity for as long as those subscribers stay subscribed. So those related party revenues, which should be capital lite, are supposed to sustain the business, we suppose. The incremental margin on that revenue must be quite high, so that could be a few more million entering into the cash flow picture, and also inventory spending falling out, together maybe bringing cash flow down to neutral levels - perhaps even net of greater R&D spend, something that we expect to occur.

Also, with commercializing a product like a logistics robot, we also expect some newer expenses

Bottom Line

In terms of dilution, there's not too much related to warrants associated to the PIPE financing. It's about a 10% effect, using the share count method. On a net new shares basis, it's probably more like 7-8%, so not very much.

But whether dilution will be needed in equity financings to supplement the $7 million in cash is unclear. At $5 million cash burn or so, the runway is a little less than 1.5 years. Q2 2026 is when the NovaArm is supposed to start bringing in money, so maybe there's not too much to worry about. Moreover, if they no longer sell their security cameras at some point this year, then they will be getting a solid $3 million+ inflow from that, without needing to spend on inventory which was the biggest cash outflow. Therefore, the cash needs should shrink, but again this is ignoring possibly significant R&D increase, where a decrease into this development year of 2025 seems unsustainable to us, and also other investments that may go into the newly announced Vietnam manufacturing footprint, which is for Kyro, and not for the soon to be released NovaArm. We are not sure what the cash draw should be for that product that is supposed to be released in Q2, but was in the advanced development phase lately.

In all, we're just not convinced we have a picture, and on the face of it there is so much related party stuff going on that it's very difficult to value the company's current operations, and it would always be difficult to value their products in the pipeline, when we don't really have any idea of how competitive and useful their offering will be in the market. It's hard to even say how capitally intense commercializing the NovaArm and eventually the Kyro will be relative to their cash balances. Not inviting a buy at the moment.
